fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Your anchor fund. Manages large-cap + mid/small-cap dynamically, giving you exposure across market segments without picking winners. Great for a merchant navy officer who can pile in big when ashore.

fund.name: Nippon India Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Your bull market ticket. Ultra-low 0.2% fees, captures the fastest growing part of Indian economy (mid-caps). Since you're at sea 8 months, this runs on auto-pilot via SIP.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund- Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Your growth rocket. Small companies have explosive potential but are riskier. Low fees again. Keep this as SIP-only since small-cap is too volatile for lump sums.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Active small-cap manager to diversify beyond index. Bandhan has 23.7% 5-year returns and ₹8,900 crore AUM (super safe size). Picks companies the index might miss.

Insurance against market crashes. Gold moved sideways avg 0.8% yearly vs stocks' 12%. Helps when your ship captain friends are betting on a brutal 2024 election. 0.4% fees, tax-free gains after 12 months.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera or Groww (free, no paperwork). Add PAN, bank, Aadhaar. Step 2: Transfer ₹8L to your bank, trigger a one-time SIP set-up for each fund in the plan. Step 3: Start the lumpsum: ₹8L to HDFC Flexi Cap, ₹5L to Nippon Midcap Index. Step 4: Set up recurring SIPs: ₹24k to HDFC Flexi Cap, ₹15k to Nippon Midcap Index, ₹9k to Motilal Smallcap Index, ₹6k to Bandhan Smallcap, ₹6k to Gold ETF. Step 5: Download the account app and check once a quarter (don't panic at short dips).
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: ensure all SIPs are running smoothly from your bank account; no missed installments. Check monthly returns briefly but don't obsess (apps like Groww show this).

After 1 year: see if any fund has lagged its category dramatically (more than 5% behind). If HDFC Flexi Cap is running far below its peer group, we'd switch to another flexi cap like Parag Parikh. Otherwise, ride it out.
After 3 years: check if your land/house goal needs more speed. If yes, increase small-cap SIPs, reduce gold. If no, stay put.
After 6-7 years: we'll start parking extra cash in debt funds (6-7% steady) as retirement approaches.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year (say, in January when ghee and sambar are cheap), check if your mix has drifted. Small-cap and mid-cap go up in bull runs, gold in fear. We want to stay close to 40% flexi, 25% midcap index, 15% smallcap index, 10% active smallcap, 10% gold. If smallcap hits 20% of your portfolio, move 2% to gold. Don't fight the market — just smooth the bumps.
plan.rationale: You're 40 with 20 years ahead, comfortable with high risk (you said aggressive). This plan pumps 90% into equity (index + flexi + small-cap) because your goal is ₹8 crore — only high returns land you there. The 10% gold cushion is here so if stocks crash 40% (happens every 10 years), you don't panic and sell early. Your merchant navy life makes this perfect: you lump-sum when ashore (₹8L+), let SIPs ride during long open seas. This is age-appropriate: young enough to ride volatility, old enough to not have 20 years of student loans. Post-tax, expect ~₹3.2 lakh annual gains after 20 years (at 20.5% LTCG), but all gains are equity-taxed (12.5% after ₹1.25L/yr) — your best friend in the new regime.
risks: Markets can drop 30-40% in a year (like 2008, 2020). Your flexi and small caps will tumble harder — midcap index alone down 35% in some years. Hold steady. Past wins don't guarantee future returns, but history shows 12-14% CAGR over 20 years. If you panic and sell, you lock in losses.
Small-cap funds are volatile — they can drop 50% in a bear market. Keep 10% in active small-cap as a diversifier; if you want to reduce risk, sell some when they're up and buy gold.
Gold is slab-taxed (gains at your 20.5% slab) vs equity's 12.5%, so your after-tax returns on gold lag. But it protects your bulk equity during crashes.
New regime investors can't use ELSS for 80C, so our entire plan is equity-taxed (great!) — long-term gains after 1 year are just 12.5% above ₹1.25L/yr cutoff.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice. Past performance doesn't guarantee future returns. This plan assumes you can stomach 20-30% portfolio drops without selling. Consult a SEBI-registered advisor before investing.

aps.reasoning:
1. Passive Instrument Fraction: 65% of the equity allocation (HDFC Flexi Cap, Nippon Midcap Index, Motilal Smallcap Index) is in index funds. The remaining 35% is in active funds (HDFC Flexi Cap, Bandhan Small Cap). Gold is excluded from this metric per instructions. Score: 0.75.
2. Turnover Score: Rebalancing is annual, with no mandate for tactical sector shifting or frequent trading. However, the plan explicitly suggests switching flexi-cap funds if they lag peers after 1 year, which introduces a mild active element. Score: 0.85.
3. Cost Emphasis: Index funds are highlighted for their 'ultra-low fees,' and tax efficiency (LTCG/STCG) is discussed. However, cost is not presented as the *sole* or dominant driver (alpha/manager conviction is also valued in the flexi cap). Score: 0.4.
4. Research vs Cost: The plan relies heavily on broad market indices (Midcap 150, Smallcap 250) for the majority of exposure, implying a 'broad market' approach rather than deep fundamental stock picking. Only the flexi cap and one small-cap fund require manager selection logic. Score: 0.85.
5. Time Horizon: The plan targets a 20-year horizon for ₹8 crore, emphasizing long-term compounding and advising patience despite volatility. Score: 0.7.
6. Portfolio Activeness: The portfolio mix (40:25:15:10:10) weighted by allocation suggests a predominantly passive strategy where active beta tracks the Nifty closely (Flexi/Mid/Small indices). The active components (HDFC Flexi, Bandhan Small Cap) increase the activeness, but the overwhelming weight of indexing pulls the score higher. Beta is likely close to 1.0 for the index portion. Score: 0.55.

pqs.reasoning: The plan demonstrates strong alignment with the investor's aggressive risk profile, 20-year horizon, and goal of building a massive 8Cr corpus. The 90% equity allocation is appropriate for a 40-year-old with no liabilities. It effectively accommodates the merchant navy lifestyle by combining large lump sums with SIPs. Diversification is excellent, covering large/mid cap indices, small cap, and gold. The plan is internally consistent in its rationale and risk warnings. Tax efficiency is high, correctly identifying that all equity holdings benefit from LTCG rates for a 'new_regime' investor, while acknowledging the slab tax on gold. A minor deduction is taken for not proposing an ELSS (equity-linked savings scheme) fund to capture tax benefits again, though this is less critical for new regime investors who cannot use 80C deductions anyway.
